content_en:
Patience (sabr) is one of the most frequently mentioned virtues in the Quran — the scholars have counted over ninety Quranic references to it — and al-Nawawi's chapter on sabr in Riyadh al-Salihin draws together the most essential hadith texts on this subject to produce one of the most practically useful chapters in the collection. The Quran's summary declaration on patience is comprehensive in its promise: 'Indeed, the patient will be given their reward without account' (Quran 39:10) — a promise that no other single virtue in the Quran receives in quite the same form.

Al-Nawawi presents the famous hadith in which the Prophet ﷺ declares: 'Wonderful is the affair of the believer, for his affairs are all good, and this is not the case for anyone except the believer. If good times come to him, he is thankful, and that is good for him. If difficulty comes to him, he is patient, and that is good for him.' (Muslim.) This hadith encapsulates the Islamic theology of patient acceptance: for the believer who truly trusts in Allah and understands the scope of divine wisdom, no experience — however painful in the moment — is purely negative. Every difficulty is an opportunity for patience, and patience is one of the highest virtues.

The chapter includes the extraordinary hadith about the blind Companion about whom the Prophet ﷺ conveyed: 'If I take away the sight of My servant's two eyes, and he remains patient and seeks My reward, I will compensate him with nothing less than Paradise.' (Al-Bukhari.) This direct divine statement — conveyed through prophetic revelation — establishes that physical disability and suffering, when met with genuine patience and the turning of the heart toward Allah rather than away from Him, is not merely a trial to be endured but a path to the highest reward.

Al-Nawawi also includes the comprehensive hadith in which the Prophet ﷺ says: 'No fatigue, illness, anxiety, sorrow, harm, or sadness afflicts a Muslim, even if it were the prick he receives from a thorn, but that Allah expiates some of his sins for it.' (Al-Bukhari and Muslim.) This narration establishes that the scope of divine compensation for patient endurance extends to the smallest inconveniences of daily life — a truth that, when genuinely internalized, transforms the entire texture of the believer's relationship with difficulty. Every moment of discomfort becomes a moment of spiritual accounting in the believer's favor.

The practical lesson al-Nawawi draws from these hadiths is that patience must be cultivated before trials arrive — a person who has not practiced patience in small things will find it very difficult to access it when a major trial strikes. The regular practice of fasting, the deliberate acceptance of small inconveniences without complaint, and the consistent practice of seeking refuge in Allah's words ('Inna lillahi wa inna ilayhi raji'un — Indeed, to Allah we belong and to Him we shall return,' Quran 2:156) in moments of difficulty are all forms of training in the virtue of sabr that prepare the soul for the greater tests that life inevitably brings.
